blurb:
Cody got adopted once already. His family loved him. It just didn't work out because they couldn't keep up with him. That's not a knock on them. Cattle dog mixes are wired to move, and at three years old and 33 pounds, Cody has a lot of moving to do.

He's been through the Prison Pups immersive training program, went to doggie daycare with his previous family and thrived, and gets described by volunteers as a strong, lovable guy with a sweet nature. He's mouthy in the playful way that cattle dog mixes are, not in any concerning way. He calmed right down during his shelter bath when the rubs and scratches started. There's a real sweetness in there underneath all that energy.

He was friendly with dogs he met at the shelter and played well with a larger dog in a foster home. No toddlers, just because he's strong and moves fast and could easily knock one over without meaning to. Older kids should be fine. Cats are unknown.

If you run, hike, have a yard, or already have an active dog at home who needs a buddy, Cody is worth a serious look. Reach out to Mostly Mutts through their website to set up a meet and greet.
scoring_notes:
• Active-lifestyle match for runners and hikers gives the newsletter a targeted, high-conversion pitch to an engaged reader segment
• Returned-but-thriving arc with Prison Pups training adds credibility and reframes the return as a fit issue, not a flaw
• Previous adoption and return with no current foster placement suggests a moderate wait with enough urgency to feature

blurb:
Cecelia was adopted from Mostly Mutts, returned after nine months, and came back scared and shut down. That's a hard thing to read. But here's where things stand now: she's in a foster home, she's doing great, and her whole body wags when she's around people she trusts.

She's a three-year-old terrier and boxer mix, about 37 pounds, and she is working through some real fear of new people, especially men she doesn't know yet. She'll snap as a warning if someone grabs at her or reaches over her head before she's ready. That's honest. But her foster family also says she's the perfect housemate, and once she trusts you, she crawls into your lap because she has apparently decided she is a lap dog. That's the dog you get when you put in the time.

She does best as an only dog at home. Cats are unknown. Mostly Mutts recommends calmer kids 15 and older given her history with new people. She's in their training program and has already learned sit, stay, come, and leave it.

This one needs a patient person. Not a project person, not a fixer. Just someone who will let her set the pace and not take it personally in the early weeks. If that sounds like you, reach out to Mostly Mutts through their website.
scoring_notes:
• Her comeback story from scared and shut down to whole-body wags is the most emotionally powerful arc in this entire group
• The blurb's direct, unsentimental honesty about her needs will build trust with exactly the right reader and drive quality inquiries
• Returned after nine months, now rebuilding in foster care with a narrow placement profile signals she has been in the system long enough to need a spotlight

blurb:
Eight years old and she still makes the cutest little noises in her sleep, feet kicking like she's chasing something good. That's Glynnis. She's a medium-sized terrier mix who came in through animal control and has spent time in foster homes, a prison pups training program, and basically every situation the rescue could throw at her. She handled all of it.

Volunteers call her sweet and precious, which sounds like something people say about every dog until you hear the foster's actual words: "the sweetest dog ever." She melts into people for scratches, gives kisses like it's her job, and will alert you when someone comes to the door. Gentle, cuddly, easy-going. At 41 pounds and eight years old, she's not looking to run a marathon. She just wants to be near her people.

She does best with male dogs only, and she can be a vocal player, so the right canine roommate matters. She chased a cat in one foster home, so cats are a no. Kids ten and up are a good fit. She's also completed the Prison Pups immersive training program, so she's got real skills.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw, and you'll want to reach out through their website to get the process started. Bring your male dog for a meet and greet if you've got one at home.
scoring_notes:
• Prison Pups training credential is a standout hook that signals real investment and makes her immediately newsworthy
• Senior dog who has cycled through animal control, foster homes, and a training program tells a rich, layered journey readers will feel
• Multiple placement types and a long road through the rescue system strongly suggest she has been waiting far too long for the right home

blurb:
Blackberry is about two and a half months old, weighs roughly a pound and a half, and is currently the smallest resident in the Mostly Mutts Kitty Room. He and his siblings were found outside without their mom when they were still tiny, and Mostly Mutts got them into foster care while they figured out how to be cats.

He's a tabby with the full kitten agenda: wand toys, crinkly balls, the cat tunnel, and then a lap or a pile of siblings when it's time to crash. The volunteers describe him and his siblings as sweet and playful, which tracks with everything in his profile. He's not a complicated guy. He just wants to play, get some love, and sleep somewhere warm.

He grew up alongside other cats and does great with them. If you have a cat at home who'd appreciate a small chaotic roommate, or if you're thinking about adopting two at once, Blackberry and one of his siblings would probably make that decision very easy. He's litter trained, has started his kitten vaccines, and whoever adopts him will need to finish the series.

Blackberry is at Mostly Mutts in Marietta right now. Head to their website to submit an inquiry or visit him in the Kitty Room.
shelter_hours: Please check mostlymutts.org for current hours
scoring_notes:
• Classic kitten profile with zero behavioral complexity makes him one of the easiest adoption pitches in the group
• Bonded-sibling angle opens a natural two-for-one adoption story that drives reader action
• New arrival still finishing vaccines indicates minimal shelter time and low urgency compared to others

blurb:
Someone found Blueberry and her siblings outside, no mom in sight, at just a few weeks old. Mostly Mutts stepped in, got them into foster homes, and watched them grow. Now she's about two and a half months old, roughly two pounds, and living in the Kitty Room at the shelter where the volunteers are already completely smitten with her.

She goes after wand toys and crinkly balls, ducks in and out of the cat tunnel for hide-and-seek with her siblings, and then crashes in a warm lap when she's burned through her energy. That's basically her whole day and she seems very happy with that arrangement.

She grew up with her siblings so she's genuinely good with other cats. If you've been thinking about getting a second cat, or if you already have one at home who'd enjoy a playmate, she'd slide right in. She's litter trained and has started her kitten vaccines. Whoever adopts her will need to finish out the vaccine series, which Mostly Mutts will walk you through.

Blueberry is available now at Mostly Mutts in Marietta. You can submit an inquiry on their website or stop by to meet her in the Kitty Room.
shelter_hours: Please check mostlymutts.org for current hours
scoring_notes:
• Kitten energy and playful personality make her an easy, high-appeal adoption for a broad audience
• Orphaned-sibling origin adds a soft emotional hook without requiring a complicated backstory
• Recently arrived and still mid-vaccine series signals she has not been waiting long at all

blurb:
Twiggy has already done this once. She got adopted from Mostly Mutts, settled into a home, and had a good run for several years. Then another cat joined the household and things went sideways. Not her fault. Just a bad match. Now she's back, decompressing in a foster home, and figuring out what chapter two looks like.

She's a seven-year-old tortoiseshell, about 12 pounds, and by all accounts she's a cat who loves people once she trusts you. She was pretty guarded when she first came back in, which makes complete sense. Her whole world got upended. But she's settling down, and the foster home is giving her the time and space to do that.

She'd do best as your only cat. She's told us that much clearly. A dog might work depending on the dog, but a cat-free home where she's the center of attention is really what she's after. Older kids who know how to give a cat her space would be fine too. She's vaccinated, spayed, microchipped, and combo tested negative. The adoption fee is $175.

Twiggy is being fostered through Mostly Mutts. Submit an inquiry on their website to get the conversation started, and they'll connect you with her foster family.
shelter_hours: Please check mostlymutts.org for current hours
scoring_notes:
• Second-chance narrative is deeply relatable and will resonate with readers who've experienced life disruptions
• Low-maintenance placement needs (only-cat home) make her a realistic fit for many households
• Returned adult cat with time in foster limbo signals she's been waiting long enough to warrant urgency
